answer:
In Allama Iqbal's *Khitāb ba Jawānān-e-Islām* ("An Address to the Muslim Youth"), the young Muslim man is most sternly demanded to cease being "all talk" and inert, instead embodying the dynamic, world-conquering action of his forebears: "You can have no true kinship with your forefathers, for you are all talk, they were all action; you are static, they were ever-moving."[^1]

In Manusmṛti Book 2, which extensively details the *brahmacārin* (celibate Vedic student), the young Brahmana is most stringently demanded to maintain absolute sexual continence amid sensory temptations: "He should sleep alone everywhere and should never spill his seed; for by spilling his seed from desire, he breaks his vow."[^2] (The chapter culminates by promising that unbroken adherence to the full regimen yields ultimate liberation: "A Brahmana who thus practices his studentship without breaking his vow goes to the highest abode and is not born here again."[^3])

Iqbal's youth—urged toward bold revivalism, imperial pride, and recovery of lost civilizational inheritance—could not realistically live up to Manu's text, which prescribes years of guru-service, alms-begging, ritual purity, total sense-restraint (even one lapse lets "wisdom flow away"[^4]), and avoidance of women, wealth, and action beyond study.[^5] Conversely, Manu's *brahmacārin*—an inward-facing ascetic vowed to Vedic study, fuel-gathering, and celibacy until marriage—could not embody Iqbal's extroverted ideal of relentless motion, world-seizing, and rejection of stasis for glory. The two paradigms are culturally and spiritually incompatible.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch.13 (*Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam*), [p-365462](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/13-khitab-ba-jawanan-e-islam/translation/#p-365462).
[^2]: *Manusmṛti*, ch.2, [p-51cabf](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-51cabf).
[^3]: *Manusmṛti*, ch.2, [p-5ac6c9](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-5ac6c9).
[^4]: *Manusmṛti*, ch.2, [p-2e26d1](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-2e26d1).
[^5]: *Manusmṛti*, ch.2 (full chapter overview).
